name: Methylprednisolone
executiveSummary: Anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant. Spinal cord injury.. Treatment of rejection in glomerulopathies (lupus, etc.).
indications:
## Main indications

Anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant. Spinal cord injury.. Treatment of rejection in glomerulopathies (lupus, etc.).

> Adjust according to institutional protocol and medical prescription.

adulto.dose: Asthma: 60–125 mg IV. MS relapse: 1 g/day x 3–5 days (protocol).

pediatrico.infusionRate: 20 to 60 min with continuous infusion pump.
pediatrico.dose: Anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant: IV: 30 mg/kg in a single dose, followed by 15 - 30 mg/kg/day or 600 mg/m²/day every 24 h for 3 days; Spinal cord injury: IV: 30 mg/kg followed by 5,4 mg/kg/hour for 23 h; Rejection treatment - glomerulopathies: IV: 10 mg/kg/dose for 3 consecutive days, maximum dose: 1 g/dose; ventricular tachycardia - silent lymphocytic myocarditis: 30 mg/kg/day in a single dose for 3 doses. See equivalent corticosteroid doses.
pediatrico.notes: Powder vial reconstitution: add only the diluent provided in the package or sterile water for injection; stable 48 h at room temperature. IV infusion: dilute in normal saline (preferably) or 5% dextrose at a recommended concentration of 2,5 - 20 mg/ml and administer over 30 - 120 minutes. IV push: maximum recommended concentration 125 mg/ml. For doses < 250 mg infuse over no less than 5 minutes; doses ≥ 250 mg infuse over no less than 30 minutes. With high doses antiacid prophylaxis may be required. See considerations for safe corticosteroid therapy. See corticosteroid update.

neonatal.dose: Restricted use; pulmonary/DAH regimens per NICU.

stability:
## General

- Reconstitute per package insert.

## Pediatric guide

- 48 h at room temperature once reconstituted.
adverseEffects:
## Adverse effects

Hypertension, hyperglycemia, peptic ulcer, myopathy, growth arrest, cataracts, osteoporosis, posterior subcapsular cataracts, cushingoid syndrome, hypokalemic alkalosis, edema, increased susceptibility to infections, osteonecrosis.
